name: Abraham's Journey
summary: Abram's migration from Ur of the Chaldeans to Haran, then into Canaan, down to Egypt during famine, and back to the hill country where he settled at Hebron.
description: Genesis traces Abram's movement along the Fertile Crescent: from Ur up the Euphrates valley to Haran, where his father Terah died, then south into Canaan with stops marked by altar-building at Shechem and Bethel. Famine drove him to Egypt, after which he returned to the Negev and Bethel before settling by the oaks of Mamre at Hebron. Years later, God tested Abraham on a mountain in the land of Moriah, traditionally identified with Jerusalem.
accuracyNote: The overall itinerary is given by Genesis; exact roads are not recorded. The route shown follows the caravan corridors of the second millennium BC (historical reconstruction). The Damascus waypoint is a traditional inference from Genesis 15:2.

stops.name: Haran
stops.note: Settled until Terah's death; the call renewed

name: The Exodus Route
summary: Israel's deliverance from Egypt: from Rameses through the sea, south to Mount Sinai, north to Kadesh-barnea, and after forty years around Edom to the plains of Moab.
description: Numbers 33 preserves the itinerary of the Exodus in station lists, though most stations cannot be located with certainty. The route shown is the traditional southern route: out of the eastern Nile delta, across the sea, down the west coast of the Sinai peninsula to Jebel Musa (the traditional Mount Sinai), north to Kadesh-barnea where the spies rebelled, and finally — after the wilderness years — around Edom by way of Ezion-geber to the plains of Moab opposite Jericho, where Moses viewed the land from Mount Nebo.
accuracyNote: Heavily debated. The crossing site is disputed (proposals range from the Bitter Lakes region to the Gulf of Suez; a minority argue for the Gulf of Aqaba). Mount Sinai's identification with Jebel Musa is traditional (Jebel al-Lawz and other candidates have scholarly advocates). Kadesh-barnea's identification with Ein el-Qudeirat is a widely held scholarly opinion. Dates follow the traditional early chronology (Exodus approx. 1446 BC).

stops.name: Rameses (Qantir)
stops.note: Departure on the night of Passover

stops.name: The sea crossing
stops.note: Site disputed; shown near the Gulf of Suez

stops.name: Rephidim (Wadi Feiran)
stops.note: Water from the rock; battle with Amalek

stops.name: Mount Sinai (Jebel Musa)
stops.note: The covenant and the law; nearly a year encamped

stops.name: Kadesh-barnea
stops.note: The twelve spies; the generation condemned to the wilderness (Ein el-Qudeirat, scholarly identification)

stops.name: Ezion-geber
stops.note: Around Edom after the wilderness years

stops.name: Mount Nebo
stops.note: Moses views the land and dies

name: The Conquest of Canaan
summary: Joshua's campaigns: crossing the Jordan, the fall of Jericho and Ai, the covenant at Shechem, the southern campaign after Gibeon, and the northern campaign against Hazor.
description: The conquest unfolds in three movements. Israel crossed the Jordan opposite Jericho and established a base at Gilgal; Jericho and Ai fell, and the covenant was renewed at Mount Ebal by Shechem. The Gibeonite treaty then drew Israel into the southern campaign — the long day of battle at Gibeon and the descent of Beth-horon, then the Shephelah strongholds of Azekah, Lachish, and Eglon, and up to Hebron and Debir. Finally the northern coalition under the king of Hazor was defeated by the waters of Merom, and Hazor burned. The land was allotted at Shiloh, and Joshua's farewell covenant was made at Shechem.
accuracyNote: City identifications follow mainstream historical geography (e.g. Lachish = Tell ed-Duweir, Hazor = Tell el-Qedah); Ai (et-Tell) and the waters of Merom are debated. The date approx. 1406–1375 BC follows the traditional early chronology.

stops.name: Plains of Moab (Shittim)
stops.note: Camp before the crossing; spies sent

stops.name: Jordan crossing
stops.note: The waters cut off; twelve memorial stones

stops.name: Ai (et-Tell)
stops.note: Defeat, then victory by ambush (identification debated)

stops.name: Gibeon
stops.note: Treaty by deception; the long day's battle

stops.name: Shechem (farewell)
stops.note: Joshua's covenant: choose whom you will serve

name: David's Kingdom
summary: The united kingdom under David: Israel and Judah with subdued neighbors — Philistia checked, Moab, Edom, Ammon, and Aram-Damascus made tributary.
description: David reigned seven years at Hebron and thirty-three at Jerusalem, which he took from the Jebusites and made his capital. 2 Samuel 8 summarizes the wars that secured the kingdom: the Philistines subdued, Moab made tributary, Edom garrisoned, Ammon defeated at Rabbah, and Aram-Damascus brought under tribute after the defeat of Hadadezer of Zobah. Under Solomon this dominion was described as stretching from the Euphrates to the border of Egypt (1 Kings 4:21).
accuracyNote: The shaded area is an approximate historical reconstruction of the core kingdom with directly subdued territories (approx. 1010–970 BC); ancient borders were zones of control, not lines. The wider sphere of tribute toward the Euphrates is described in the text but not shaded. Archaeological evidence: the Tel Dan stele's 'House of David' attests the dynasty within about 150 years of David's reign.

regions.name: Approximate extent of David's kingdom (historical reconstruction)

stops.name: Jerusalem
stops.note: Capital from approx. 1003 BC; the ark brought up

stops.name: Hebron
stops.note: First capital, seven years over Judah

stops.name: Dan
stops.note: Traditional northern limit: 'from Dan to Beersheba'

name: Jesus' Ministry
summary: Key sites of Jesus' life and ministry: from Bethlehem and Nazareth through the Galilee ministry centered on Capernaum, to Jerusalem, the cross, and the resurrection.
description: The Gospels are not a travel diary — Jesus moved repeatedly between Galilee and Jerusalem for the feasts (John records several such journeys). The map therefore marks the principal sites rather than a single route: the birth at Bethlehem, boyhood at Nazareth, baptism at the Jordan, the Galilean ministry based in Capernaum with works at Cana, Nain, and Bethsaida, the withdrawal north to Caesarea Philippi and the transfiguration, the raising of Lazarus at Bethany, and the final week in Jerusalem.
accuracyNote: Sites marked 'traditional' rest on early church identification (e.g. the baptism site at al-Maghtas, Mount Tabor for the transfiguration — some scholars prefer Mount Hermon's slopes; Emmaus' location is debated). Dates approx. AD 26–30/33.

stops.name: Nazareth
stops.note: Boyhood and early life; rejection at the synagogue

stops.note: Water to wine, the first sign

stops.note: The final week: cross and resurrection

name: Paul's Missionary Journeys
summary: Paul's three missionary journeys from Antioch — Cyprus and Galatia; Macedonia and Achaia; Ephesus and the Aegean — and his final voyage under guard to Rome.
description: From the church at Antioch Paul was sent out three times. The first journey (with Barnabas, approx. AD 47–48) evangelized Cyprus and the cities of southern Galatia. The second (with Silas, approx. AD 49–52) revisited Galatia, then crossed to Macedonia — Philippi, Thessalonica, Berea — and on to Athens and eighteen months at Corinth. The third (approx. AD 53–57) centered on three years at Ephesus before a farewell tour through Macedonia and Greece and the voyage to Jerusalem. Arrested there, Paul appealed to Caesar and sailed for Rome (approx. AD 59–60), shipwrecked on Malta on the way.
accuracyNote: City locations are well established; sea lanes and roads (e.g. the Via Egnatia) are drawn schematically between ports and cities. Dates are approximate, anchored on the Gallio inscription at Delphi (Acts 18:12, approx. AD 51–52).

name: The Seven Churches of Revelation
summary: The seven churches of the province of Asia addressed by the risen Christ through John: Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea.
description: Exiled on Patmos, John was commanded to write what he saw to seven churches of Asia. Their order in Revelation traces a natural courier circuit: north from Ephesus up the coast to Smyrna and Pergamum, then inland and south through Thyatira, Sardis, and Philadelphia to Laodicea in the Lycus valley. Each letter speaks to the city's circumstances — Laodicea's lukewarm water, Sardis' reputation without life, Smyrna's poverty and coming persecution.
accuracyNote: All seven sites are securely identified (modern Selçuk, İzmir, Bergama, Akhisar, Sart, Alaşehir, and near Denizli in Türkiye). The connecting line marks the letter order — a plausible Roman-road delivery circuit (scholarly opinion), not a route recorded in the text.

stops.name: Patmos
stops.note: John in exile; the vision received

stops.name: Thyatira
stops.note: Love and faith, but Jezebel tolerated

stops.name: Sardis
stops.note: A name of being alive, yet dead

stops.name: Philadelphia
stops.note: An open door no one can shut
